## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** — if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) — if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** — for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here — don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling — if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory — the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch — load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools — that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category — the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) → tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) → tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** → tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier — bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety — treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading — hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies — verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.

**PR #43 (T-004, star volume):**
- PR clones a sphere `ModelEntity` per star (up to 1200 individual entities — heavy) with no twinkle animation.
- **Main already** has the full system: `StarComponent`/`StarVolumeComponent`, a `StarAnimationSystem` driving per-star twinkle and global yaw off `simTime` (satisfies the §5 one-clock rule), which the PR completely lacks.

Both PRs conflict (`DIRTY`/`CONFLICTING`) precisely because `main` overwrote these same TODO stubs with better code. They look like **stale Ollama-farm outputs** — same `Entire-Session` trailer on both, created ~16:30–16:41, after/around when the real work landed on main.

### Recommendation
Close both PRs (they `Closes #3` and `Closes #4` — verify those tickets are actually satisfied by main before closing so the issues don't get wrongly reopened), rather than attempting to resolve conflicts. Merging either would *regress* the depth work — and per your CLAUDE.md, M1 "prove depth" is a hard gate.

Both issues are **already closed** (`stateReason: COMPLETED`, closed 17:04 today — *after* the PRs were created at 16:30/16:41, consistent with the real work landing on main and the tickets being marked done then). So there's nothing left to close. Here's the verification of whether that closed state is *correct*:

## T-003 / Issue #3 — far backdrop ✅ (met, one deferred detail)
| Requirement | On main | |
|---|---|---|
| Giant inward sphere ~1000 m | `generateSphere(radius: 1000)` | ✅ |
| `scale.z = -1` to face inward | `e.scale.z = -1` | ✅ |
| `UnlitMaterial` | ✅ | ✅ |
| Equirect/cubemap **placeholder texture** | Uses a solid near-black void **color**, not a texture | ⚠️ deferred |

The "no visible pole pinch" acceptance is trivially satisfied by a flat color, and the actual stars come from L3 — so functionally it's a valid backdrop. But the equirectangular texture is still an explicit `// TODO … (§4)` in the code. Correctly closed as the *stub-implementation* milestone; the texture swap is genuine remaining work.

## T-004 / Issue #4 — star volume ✅ (met, one perf caveat)
| Requirement | On main | |
|---|---|---|
| N points across ~2–200 m | `minRadius 2 … maxRadius 200`, 3000 stars | ✅ |
| 3D shell, density falloff (not one radius) | `sqrt(rng.unit())` falloff — denser near | ✅ |
| Vary brightness/size by distance | scale from `distFraction` + per-star twinkle | ✅ |
| Deterministic from `gen.starStream()` (§5) | ✅ seeded stream | ✅ |
| **Prefer** `LowLevelMesh` instanced points | 3000 individual `ModelEntity`s instead | ⚠️ deviation |

All hard requirements met; "LowLevelMesh instanced points" was worded as a *preference* ("prefer"), and main used per-entity spheres. For a `risk:high` ticket, 3000 separate entities is a real perf question worth watching, but it doesn't block the functional/parallax acceptance.

## Bottom line
Both issues are **legitimately closed** — the code on main satisfies every hard acceptance criterion. Two items remain as follow-up work (not regressions):
1. **#3** — swap the placeholder color for a real equirectangular star texture (already a `// TODO §4`).
2. **#4** — consider migrating 3000 `ModelEntity`s to `LowLevelMesh` instancing if the M1 perf sign-off flags it.

Both also carry the visual/perf acceptance boxes (`no pole pinch`, `parallax`, `xcodegen generate`) that need a **headset sign-off** — those are `agent:needs-human` in spirit and can't be verified from code alone. Want me to file those two follow-ups as fresh tickets so they don't get lost now that #3/#4 are closed?

content.text:
Found it — this is the smoking gun:

- GitHub repo `darthzen/float` was **created at 14:24:41Z**
- Mirror was registered **14:26:46Z** — just **2 minutes later**, when the repo was essentially empty
- But the real content was pushed later: last push **18:32:17Z** (~4 hours after the mirror cloned)
- **GitHub's default branch is `entire/checkpoints/v1`**, and the dashboard reads the mirror's clone of that default branch

So the mirror did its initial clone against a freshly-created, empty repo and never backfilled the commits/checkpoints that landed afterward. The session/checkpoint *activity* plane synced (separate agent-hook path — that's why `entire activity` shows float data), but the **git-mirror plane is stuck at the empty initial clone**. The fix is to force a fresh clone by re-registering the mirror now that GitHub has the full history.

Let me check the remove syntax, then re-register.

content.content:
===== mirror remove --help =====
Removes a mirror placement for a GitHub repo from the target cluster. Other clusters' placements of the same upstream are unaffected. The cluster-host defaults to aws-us-east-2.entire.io when omitted.

Usage:
  entire repo mirror remove <github-url> [cluster-host] [flags]

Examples:
  entire repo mirror remove github.com/octocat/hello-world

Flags:
  -h, --help   help for remove

Global Flags:
      --json   Output raw JSON instead of a table

===== mirror create --help =====
With no arguments, launches an interactive wizard: pick repos to mirror, pick one or more regions, then creates every (repo, region) mirror in parallel and prints the clone URLs.

With a <github-url>, registers a mirror placement for that repo on the target cluster, then waits for the initial GitHub→EntireDB clone to finish so `git clone` works on return. Pass --no-wait to return as soon as the placement is registered. Idempotent on (upstream, cluster). When the cluster-host is omitted, an interactive terminal offers the available clusters as a picker; non-interactive runs default to aws-us-east-2.entire.io.

Usage:
  entire repo mirror create [github-url] [cluster-host] [flags]

Examples:
  entire repo mirror create
  entire repo mirror create github.com/octocat/hello-world
  entire repo mirror create github.com/octocat/hello-world aws-us-east-2.entire.io

Flags:

---
name: entire-mirror-registration
description: "An entire.io repo only shows on the dashboard once it's a registered cloud MIRROR — enabling + pushing + capturing sessions is not enough; run `entire repo mirror create` as part of every enable"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project

  modified: 2026-07-19T18:28:25.586Z
---

The entire.io dashboard lists only repos that are registered as GitHub **mirrors** in the cloud project. A repo can be fully healthy locally — `.entire/settings.json` enabled, commits pushed to `darthzen/<repo>`, Claude Code hooks firing, sessions + checkpoints captured — and still be **absent from the dashboard** if it was never mirrored. This is exactly what happened to `darthzen/float` on 2026-07-19; every other `darthzen/*` repo showed `mirrored` while float showed `available`.

**Diagnose:**
- `entire repo mirror list --show-available` → each repo shows `mirrored` (on dashboard) or `available` (not yet). GitHub-App `admin`/access is required and was already present for all darthzen repos.
- `entire repo list <project-ULID>` → dashboard-visible repos; find the project ULID via `entire project list`.

**Fix (idempotent, non-destructive):**
```
entire repo mirror create github.com/darthzen/<repo>
```
Registers the placement on `aws-us-east-2.entire.io` and runs the initial GitHub→EntireDB clone. The repo appears on the dashboard immediately, including any pre-existing `[imported]` checkpoints.

**Why:** mirror registration and session capture are independent. Missing mirror = repo absent entirely; degenerate transcript = session content thin but repo still shows (see [[entire-xcode-transcript]]).

**How to apply — standard procedure when enabling entire on a NEW repo** (now also written into Float's CLAUDE.md entire workflow): after `entire enable` + `entire agent add claude-code`, immediately run `entire repo mirror create github.com/darthzen/<repo>` and confirm with `entire repo mirror list --show-available` that it flipped to `mirrored`. Enabling alone does not register the mirror. Related: [[entire-xcode-transcript]], [[farming-proxy-routing]].

content.text: I'll add a new memory for this distinct failure mode (mirror registered too early → empty clone), and cross-link it.

input.content:
---
name: entire-mirror-empty-clone
description: "An entire.io mirror registered right after the GitHub repo was created shows 0 commits/0 checkpoints on the dashboard forever — the initial clone captured an empty repo and never backfills; fix = remove + re-create the mirror"
metadata:
  node_type: memory
  type: project
---

Distinct from [[entire-mirror-registration]] (repo *absent* because never mirrored). Here the repo IS `mirrored` and `status: ready`, but the dashboard shows **0 commits and 0 checkpoints**.

**Root cause (seen on `darthzen/float`, 2026-07-19):** the mirror's initial GitHub→EntireDB clone runs at registration time and captures whatever is on GitHub *then*. If the mirror is registered within minutes of `gh repo create`, before any real commits are pushed, it clones an empty repo. The git-mirror plane never backfills the commits pushed afterward, so the dashboard stays at zero. Confirmed by timestamps: GitHub repo `createdAt` 14:24:41Z, mirror created 14:26:46Z (2 min later), but the actual commits were pushed up to `pushedAt` 18:32:17Z (~4h later).

**Key tell — two independent data planes:** the session/checkpoint *activity* plane (agent hooks → cloud) syncs fine and is NOT affected. `entire activity` will show the repo WITH checkpoints (float showed 5) even while the git-mirror dashboard shows 0. So "activity has data but dashboard commits/checkpoints = 0" ⇒ it's the git-mirror clone that's stale, not session capture.

**Diagnose:**
- `git ls-remote --heads entire://aws-us-east-2.entire.io/gh/darthzen/<repo>` vs `git ls-remote --heads origin` — if the entire mirror is missing branches / behind GitHub, the clone is stale.
- `gh repo view darthzen/<repo> --json defaultBranchRef,createdAt,pushedAt` and compare `createdAt` to the mirror's `createdAt` (`entire repo mirror list --json`). Repo created ≈ mirror created ⇒ empty-clone trap.

**Fix (re-clones against current GitHub state):**
```
entire repo mirror remove github.com/darthzen/<repo>
entire repo mirror create github.com/darthzen/<repo>
```
`create` is idempotent on (upstream, cluster), so re-running it alone will NOT re-clone — you must `remove` first. `create` waits for the fresh clone; verify with `git ls-remote` that the mirror refs now match origin.

**How to apply:** when enabling entire on a brand-new repo, either push real commits BEFORE `entire repo mirror create`, or expect to remove+recreate once content exists. Related: [[entire-mirror-registration]], [[entire-xcode-transcript]].

===== entire api --help =====
Make an authenticated HTTP request to an Entire API and print the JSON response.

The CLI attaches the right bearer token and dials the right host for the
chosen backend, so you don't have to plumb auth yourself:

  --to core   the control plane (default): orgs, repos, mirrors, clusters, /me
  --to cell   your home entire-api cell: /me/* activity, repo aggregates

Use --jurisdiction <slug> (e.g. us, eu) to reach a specific jurisdiction's
entire-api cell instead of your home one; it implies --to cell.

<path> is the full path on that host, e.g. /api/v1/clusters. These
placeholders are filled from the current repo's origin remote:
  {owner} {repo}   the GitHub owner / repo
  {repo_id}        the repo's Entire ULID (from its mirror) — cells key on this

The method is GET unless a field/body is given (then POST); override with -X.

Usage:
  entire api <path> [flags]

Examples:
  entire api /api/v1/clusters
  entire api --to cell /api/v1/me/activity
  entire api --jurisdiction eu /api/v1/me/activity
  entire api --to cell "/api/v1/me/recap?repo={repo_id}"
  entire api -X POST /api/v1/projects -f name=demo

Flags:
  -F, --field stringArray       add a typed parameter in key=value format; true/false/null/numbers are converted (repeatable)
  -H, --header stringArray      add a request header in key:value format (repeatable)
  -h, --help                    help for api
  -i, --include                 print the response status line and headers too
      --input string            file with the request body ("-" for stdin)
  -j, --jurisdiction string     target a specific jurisdiction's cell (e.g. us, eu) instead of your home cell; implies --to cell
  -X, --method string           HTTP method (default GET, or POST when a field/body is given)
  -f, --raw-field stringArray   add a string parameter in key=value format (repeatable)
      --to string               which backend to call: core or cell (default "core")

===== entire activity --help (look for --json/--days) =====
Display your activity overview, repository breakdown, and recent sessions from entire.io.

The recent list shows your sessions by default, matching the entire.io Overview page. Pass --commits to show recent commits instead.

Usage:
  entire activity [flags]

Flags:
      --commits   Show recent commits instead of recent sessions
  -h, --help      help for activity

===== entire recap --help =====
Summarize recent checkpoint activity

Usage:
  entire recap [flags]

Flags:
      --90                   Rolling 90 days
      --agent string         Agent id to show, or all (default "all")
      --color string         Color output: auto, always, or never (default "auto")
      --day                  Today only (default)
  -h, --help                 help for recap
      --insecure-http-auth   Allow plain-HTTP auth (local dev only)
      --month                This calendar month
      --static               Print static output instead of opening the interactive recap
      --view string          Which columns to show: you, team, or both (default "both")
      --week                 Last 7 days

content.text:
The backend is actually healthy — `entire recap --week` (last 7 days, `Jul 13–20 CDT`) returns **4 sessions, 5 checkpoints, 11k tokens, repo darthzen/float, most active 2026-07-19**. So the 7-day data exists and is queryable; my mirror re-clone did **not** lose it.

Two things stand out that explain the *web* panel looking empty:
1. `transcriptTokens: 0`, `filesChanged: 0` everywhere, plus the in-tool note *"Labels require server analysis (may take a few minutes after committing)."* The web analytics charts render from those **enriched** per-checkpoint metrics, which are computed asynchronously and haven't run for the freshly re-cloned mirror yet.
2. Most of the 7-day checkpoints are from an **"Unknown" agent** (2 sessions / 4 checkpoints / 0 tokens) — the imported/degenerate transcripts (your known Xcode-transcript issue). Those never produce rich analytics; only the Claude Code sessions carry token/tool data.

Let me confirm the one thing I changed didn't break the association — whether the new and old mirror records share a repo group (which is what analytics key on):

input.command:

content.text:
Confirmed the association is intact: the new mirror record shares the **same `repoGroupId` (`01KXXKNYYVXE7D4JAGT08J2CC0`)** as the old one. Analytics aggregate on the repo group, not the individual mirror ULID — so my remove/recreate did **not** orphan anything. That's why both old and new repo_ids resolve to float's data.

## Diagnosis

**Your data is safe and present on the backend.** The "no analytic data in the last 7 days" is a *display/enrichment* problem, not data loss:

- `entire recap --week` returns real 7-day data (4 sessions, 5 checkpoints, 11k tokens, most-active 2026-07-19). The session/checkpoint counts are there.
- But the **enriched metrics the web charts actually plot** — `filesChanged`, `transcriptTokens`, labels — are all **0 / pending**. Entire computes those asynchronously server-side (the CLI even flags it: *"Labels require server analysis, may take a few minutes after committing"*). I re-cloned the mirror only ~25 min ago, so that enrichment hasn't run against the newly-populated git history yet.
- Compounding it: most of the last-7-days checkpoints are from an **"Unknown" agent** (2 sessions / 4 checkpoints / **0 tokens**) — the imported/degenerate transcripts (your known Xcode-transcript limitation). Those never produce rich analytics; only the Claude Code sessions do.

## What to do

The enrichment backfills off a **commit/checkpoint event** now that the mirror has real history. So either:

1. **Wait & re-check** — it should populate on the next server-analysis cycle. I can re-check in ~15 min.
2. **Trigger it** — you have uncommitted changes in the tree (`ImmersiveView.swift`, `DepthLayers.swift`). Your next real commit will fire a fresh checkpoint + server analysis and light up the panel. (I won't commit those without your say-so.)

Want me to re-check the backend in a bit to confirm it's filling in, or are you about to commit that working-tree work anyway (which would settle it)?
